Using the wrong image format on your website ruins loading times. Using the wrong format in print ruins your design. With all the acronyms out there — JPEG, PNG, WebP, SVG, HEIC — it’s easy to get lost.
Here is your quick, no-nonsense guide to choosing the right format for Web, iOS, Mobile and Print.
🚀 The 10-Second Decision Matrix
In a rush? Just follow these golden rules:

• Photos on Web/Mobile: WebP (better compression than JPEG at the same quality).

• Logos, Icons, and Graphics: SVG (infinitely scalable, tiny file size).

• Images with Transparency (Web/Mobile): WebP or PNG (as a fallback).

• iOS Photography (Native): HEIC (space-saving, but best within the Apple ecosystem).

• Print Media: TIFF or high-resolution JPEG (always use the CMYK color space!).
⚖️ The Core Concept: Lossy vs. Lossless
Before diving into specific use cases, you need to understand how images compress data:
Lossy Compression: Algorithms discard image data that the human eye barely notices to keep file sizes incredibly small (e.g., JPEG, WebP).
The Rule: Perfect for the web. However, avoid re-exporting a JPEG multiple times, as the quality degrades with every save.
Lossless Compression: The file is compressed mathematically without losing a single pixel of quality (e.g., PNG, TIFF).
The Rule: Ideal for graphics with text, sharp edges, or print. File sizes are significantly larger.
📱 Deep Dive by Channel
1. Websites & Mobile Web
On the web, every millisecond counts. Avoid traditional JPEGs whenever possible.

• WebP: The reigning king. It supports transparency and compresses photos much better than JPEG. It is now supported by all modern browsers.

• SVG: A must for logos and icons. Because it is essentially XML code, it stays razor-sharp on any Retina display and can even be animated using CSS.
2. iOS & the Apple Ecosystem
Apple uses its own formats to save storage space on iPhones and iPads.

• HEIC (High Efficiency Image Container): Apple’s standard for photos. It takes up half the space of a JPEG. Warning: You must convert it to WebP or JPEG before using it on Android or the web.

• PNG: Still the standard for UI elements inside iOS app development (Xcode) because iOS renders PNGs extremely efficiently.
3. Print Media
Print follows entirely different rules. Forget pixel dimensions — focus on DPI (minimum 300 DPI required) and color spaces (CMYK instead of RGB).

• TIFF: The professional standard for print. It is lossless and supports CMYK, but results in massive file sizes.

• JPEG (High-Res): Perfectly fine for printing, provided you use minimal compression (quality level 10–12).
👾 The Specialists: GIF and RAW
Some formats serve highly specific purposes outside of standard layouts:

• GIF (Graphics Interchange Format): Technically outdated (limited to 256 colors), but immortal due to animations. Unsuitable for static images. Pro Tip: For silent loops on the web, modern video formats like MP4 or WebM are often much smaller than a heavy GIF.

• RAW (The Digital Negative): Not a finished image format, but uncompressed raw data directly from a camera sensor. Essential for photo editing (e.g., Lightroom) because it preserves maximum detail in shadows and highlights. Never upload RAW files directly to the web or send them to print — always develop and export them first.
🌐 Golden Rules for Maximum Compatibility
Nothing is more frustrating than an image that fails to load for a user or client. Play it safe with these rules:

• The Web Safety Net (Fallback): While WebP is widely supported, always provide a JPEG or PNG fallback in your website HTML (using the <picture> tag) for legacy browsers.

• The Universal Exchange Format: When sending images to clients, agencies, or cross-platform users (Windows/Mac/Android), JPEG remains the safest bet. Most devices on Earth can open it.

• No HEIC on the Web: Never upload raw .heic files to your website or social media channels. Convert them first.
📋 Image format Cheat Sheet
